coredata.dc:title: Strongly blue-luminescent N-doped carbogenic dots as a tracer metal sensing probe in aqueous medium and its potential activity towards in situ Ag-nanoparticle synthesis
coredata.dc:creator: Ganguly, Sayan
Das, Poushali
Bose, Madhuparna
Mondal, Subhadip
Das, Amit Kumar
Das, N.C.
coredata.dc:description: Milligram scale one-pot synthesis of N-doped, blue luminescent, highly water soluble and short fluorescent lifetime carbogenic dots have been developed by thermal coupling of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (tris) and glycine. The size distribution is in the range of 1.5-4.5nm with a high abundance of 3nm particles. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) also confirms the TEM particle size data. The carbogenic dots show pH sensitive and excellent saline environment stability without diminishing major fluorescent character. The nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) can anchor Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution which results major drop off effect in fluorescence intensity by inner filter effect. Our as prepared CDs have a unique ability to detect Cu2+ ion with a detection limit of 0.32μM in the dynamic range of 0.4–300μM. The prepared carbon dots have also showed potential activity as a reducing agent. Here, silver nanoparticles have been synthesized without using any external rigorous reducing agent or conditions. Meanwhile our synthesis was also less time consuming and the derived carbon dots can also stabilize the nanoparticles without agglomeration up to several weeks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane
Fluorescent
Nitrogen doped carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalytic reduction to prepare silver nanoparticles (AgNPs)
TG-CDs based chemosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+
Sensitivity as a chemosensor for Cu2+ detection
Sensitivity of the TG-CDs for Cu2+ detection
Assay of TG-CDs based Cu2+ sensor in environmental water samples
Catalytic productions of TG-CDs assisted AgNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and molecular structure of the water-dispersible N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The digital photo shows the blue light emission after UV-excited water dispersion of carbon dots. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image of TG-CDs and size distribution of TG-CDs (inset) (b) Magnified TEM image of TG-CDs (c) Lattice fringe of TG-CDs with 0.237nm d-spacing (d) SAED pattern of TG-CDs (e-f) AFM images of TG-CDs (g) EDX spectrum of TG-CDs (h) FTIR of TG-CDs (i) XRD plot of TG-CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of as prepared TG-CDs (b) High-resolution XPS survey spectra of C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s region of TG-CDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum (black line) and FL-spectrum (excitation-emission) of TG-CDs (b) effect of medium pH towards TG-CDs (c) Excitation dependent fluorescence spectra of TG-CDs (d) the typical luminescence decay curve for the UV emission measured at room temperature (e) Effect of salinity towards TG-CDs in aqueous dispersions (f) Effect of precursor ratios on the fluorescence intensity of the TG-CDs.
(a) The stability of the TG-CDs. (b) The effect of dissolved oxygen of the TG-CDs.
Schematic representation of the growth mechanism of TG-CDs.

(a) FL spectra of NCDs dispersion in the presence of different Cu2+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.4, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 18, 40, 80, 150, and 300μM) (b) Time-dependent PL spectra of TG-CDs −Cu2+ dispersion at room temperature (c) FL intensity response of TG-CDs to the concentration of the Cu2+ ions (inset: FL intensity of TG-CDs versus the Cu2+ concentrations from 0 to 12μM) (d) FL responses of 14.3μgmL−1 TG-CDs in the absence (pink in colour version, light black in black and white version) and presence (blue in colour version, dark black in black and white version) of 10μM copper ions at different pH values (e) Selectivity of the TG-CDs as sensor for Cu2+ ions over other ions in pH 4 PBS solution. The concentrations of TG-CDs and metal ions were 16.8μgmL−1 and 50μM, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) FL spectra of TG-CDs dispersion (river water) in the presence of different Cu2+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 100μM) (b) Stern-Volmer plot for the TG-CDs −Cu2+ dispersion for 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 100μM Cu2+ solutions (inset: linear relationship between the F/F 0 and Cu2+ concentration (0–15μM) (c) Comparison among the FL spectra of pure TG-CDs, after quenching and after restore by Na2EDTA salt.

(a) UV−vis spectra of the reaction mixtures: (i) AgNO3 (6×10−2 mM) incubating with tris-glycine aqueous solution (45mgL−1) in the dark, (ii) AgNO3 (6×10−2 mM) incubating with tris-glycine aqueous solution (45mgL−1) in the normal light, (ii) AgNO3 (6×10−2 mM) incubating with TG-CDs (45mgL −1) in the light, and (iii) AgNO3 (6×10−2 mM) incubating with TG-CDs (45mgL−1) in the dark; Inset: Photographs of sample. (b) HRTEM images of the Ag-NPs/TG-Cds nano-composites made from AgNO3 (6×10−2 mM) and C-dots (45mgL−1) in 0.01M NaOH incubating at 50°C for 5min. Inset: magnified HRTEM images of single AgNP with fringe spacing.

(a) UV–vis spectra of the AgNPs-TG-CDs nano-composites after incubating AgNO3 (6×10−2 mM) with TG-CDs (45 −106mgL−1) in 0.01M NaOH at 50°C for 5min. Inset: Photographs of sample solutions with 45 −106mgL−1 TG-CDs (b) Alkaline environment −dependent absorbance at 410nm for the derived AgNPs.
The linear calibration plot of RLS intensity vs Ag+ concentration with constant concentration of TG-CDs of 45mgL−1, 0.01M NaOH in 50°C for 5min
Comparison of various fluorescent sensor probes with their detection limit for Cu2+ ion detection.

row.entry: Human blood serum, commercial tea and wheat flour
Monitoring of bioavailable heavy metal ions

head.ce:title: Strongly blue-luminescent N-doped carbogenic dots as a tracer metal sensing probe in aqueous medium and its potential activity towards in situ Ag-nanoparticle synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Milligram scale one-pot synthesis of N-doped, blue luminescent, highly water soluble and short fluorescent lifetime carbogenic dots have been developed by thermal coupling of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (tris) and glycine. The size distribution is in the range of 1.5-4.5nm with a high abundance of 3nm particles. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) also confirms the TEM particle size data. The carbogenic dots show pH sensitive and excellent saline environment stability without diminishing major fluorescent character. The nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) can anchor Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution which results major drop off effect in fluorescence intensity by inner filter effect. Our as prepared CDs have a unique ability to detect Cu2+ ion with a detection limit of 0.32μM in the dynamic range of 0.4–300μM. The prepared carbon dots have also showed potential activity as a reducing agent. Here, silver nanoparticles have been synthesized without using any external rigorous reducing agent or conditions. Meanwhile our synthesis was also less time consuming and the derived carbon dots can also stabilize the nanoparticles without agglomeration up to several weeks.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbogenicdots (often called carbon quantum dots or C-dots), an emerging member belongs to carbon nanomaterial community, were first obtained as sludge during refining of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 and via laser ablation of graphite powder and cement in 2006 [1,2]. Carbon dots are nanomaterials of carbon family with a size of less than 10nm [3]. These are basically physiologically benign and inexpensive materials which ensued in various applications in biological leveling, optoelectronics, potential catalytic technology and biomedicines [4–10]. Photoluminescent carbon dots are superior to traditional semiconductor carbon dots and organic dyes in the area of water solubility, functionality and relatively high quantum yields. Photoluminescent carbon dots are superior to traditional semiconductor carbon dots and organic dyes in the area of water solubility, functionality and relatively high quantum yields [11]. Water solubilization of carbon as nanodots exhibits strong luminescence for which they are addressed as carbon nanolights [12–14]. Many researchers developed many routes to form carbon dots like high energy ion beam radiation and laser ablation techniques [15]. But one major drawback for these techniques is all are expensive, energy intensive. To minimize such problems relatively low cost, different chemical methods were adapted to synthesize carbon dots. Acid oxidized gas shoot or activated carbon, oxidized candle shoot were used to develop carbon dots in a low cost [16,17]. Carbon dots from green source are also practiced immensely due to abundant source, low cost and water solubility. De et al. developed banana leaf based extract for preparing carbon dots [18]. Other viable natural sources were used for carbon dots synthesis with improved luminescent property [19–22]. Carbonization of glucose, sucrose, diols, triols, ascorbic acid, amino acids etc. has also attained substantial attention for the production of fluorescent carbon dots. But maximum processes demand multistep operations or strong acid treatment followed by using of surface passivation agents [23–26].

In very recent era, fluorescent carbon dots have played as chemosensors for the detection of heavy metal ions, even at as a tracer element. These carbon dots have significant intrinsic sensitivity, chemoselectivity towards metal ions, rapid detection and real time monitoring propensity [27–38]. Copper ion plays a significant role in chemical, environmental and biological systems. It is also an essential element for animals and humans in trace amount [39]. But according to U.S. environmental protection agency (EPA), it becomes toxic if the permissible level in water is above 1.3ppm or approx. 20μM [40]. The tolerance limit of copper ion (+2) in human blood is 15.7-23.6μM [41]. But above the permissible level of the Cu2+ ion in blood causes gastrointestinal disturbance or severe liver and kidney damage upon prolonged exposure [29]. There are several methods also used to detect Cu2+ ions, viz. atomic absorption spectroscopy[42], inductively coupled mass spectroscopy [43] and electrochemical methods [44]. But the main drawback in such methods are that, these are highly expensive, sophisticated and sometimes time consuming also. Hence, to qualify and quantify the Cu2+ ion in environment and ecosystem, analytical methods to sense Cu2+ ion become very desirable. There are many fluorescent sensing probes already have been published for sensing of copper ion (+2), such as organic dye or organic complex [45–48], carbogenic dots [49], inorganic quantum dots [50], etc. In this context it is highly worthy to detect heavy metal ions in aqueous medium with low cost and rapid-sensing of Cu2+ ions in biological, environmental and toxicological samples with avoiding of those aforementioned difficulties.

Herein, a single-step eloquent approached has been developed to prepare N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with blue emitting fluorescence. The fluorescence activity was quenched by several metals ions, but a drastic quenching was occurred in presence of Cu2+ ions. This attempt might provide a new pathway in Cu2+ ion detection also in tracer amount in aqueous medium.

Glycine and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (tris) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Phenol, methanol and hydrogen peroxide was procured from Merck, India. All chemicals used here were reagent grades.

Tris-glycine C-dots were synthesized by thermal coupling of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane i.e. tris and glycine. Initially 1g of glycine (13.32mmol) mixed with equivalent amount of tris aqueous solution (2mL). Then the solution was evaporated to dryness at 220°C in a muffle furnace. A sticky brownish mass was obtained after water evaporation after drying it at 80°C for 72h. The solid mass was crushed into fine powder and extracted with hot water. The supernatant was collected after centrifuged it at 12000rpm for 30min. The solvent was evaporated again from the solution at room temperature under vacuum to obtain fluorescent C-dots with mass yield of 54%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic reduction to prepare silver nanoparticles (AgNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare AgNPs, an aqueous solution of silver nitrate (AgNO3) having concentration of 8×10−2 molL−1 was incubated with C −dot aqueous dispersion (35mgL−1), and NaOH (0.01M) in 50°C in a water bath for 5min. The acidity of the mixture was maintained at pH=4 for the uniform growth of AgNPs.

The FTIR spectrum of C-dots was performed on a FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, model-Spectrum-2, Singapore) using KBr pellet made by mixing KBr with fine powder of the C-dots (10:1 wt ratio of KBr to C-dots). Each FTIR spectrum was scanned with a resolution of 4cm−1 and 16 scans in the wavelength range of 500−4000cm−1 at room temperature. The elemental composition was determined electron dispersive X-ray study (EDX, INCA PentaFET x3, Oxford Instrument UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) experiment was performed in Bruker, D2 phaser with SSD160 detector. The morphology and the microstructure were evaluated by high resolution transmission electron microscope, HRTEM (JEOL, Japan operating voltage 200kV with filament LaB6). Surface topography of TG-CDs was scanned by Agilent 5500 scanning probe microscope (resonance frequency of tip is 146–236kHz and force constant was kept at 48 Nm−1) after drop casting of TG-CDs on Silicon wafer as substrate. The images were taken in non-contact tapping mode with a scan area of 500×500nm2. Raman measurement was measured using spectrometer (MODEL T64000 (Make JobinYvon Horiba, France). Argon-Krypton mixed ion gas laser (MODEL 2018 RM) was used as an excitation source. Thermoelectric cooled front illuminated 1024 256 CCD, MODEL Synpse TM (Make JobinYvon Horiba, France) was used as the detector. The X-ray photoelectron spectra have been performed in PHI 5000 Versa Probe II scanning X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), with a monochromatic Al Kα source (1486eV). A UV–vis spectrum of C-dots in aqueous solution was recorded using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Singapore PTE Ltd., Model lambda 35). The zeta potential was recorded on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, Manchester, U.K.) Fluorescence measurements and resonance light scattering were analyzed on a Fluoromax_4C_1052D_4312_FM spectrofluorometer in aqueous solution. The fluorescence lifetime was carried out on a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer FL3-P-TCSPC (Horiba Jobin Jvon, France).The quantum yield of C-dots were calculated at an excitation wavelength of 350nm by the equation given below [51],
(1)
Q CD
=
Q R
.
I CD
I R
.
A R
A CD
.
η CD 2
η R 2
Where ‘Q’ represents the quantum yield, ‘I’ is the intensity of luminescent spectra, ‘A’ is the absorbance at particular exited wavelength and ‘η’ is the refractive index of the solvent used. Here, in our study we have chosen quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 as standard and its quantum yield (Q CD) is known to be 54% in 0.1M H2SO4 solution. The subscripts ‘CD’ for C-dots and ‘R’ for reference are used in this equation.

A simple approach for the preparation of single step photoluminescent water soluble NCDs from organic precursors, like 2-amino-2-hydroxymethylpropane-1, 3-diol (tris) and glycine (gly) has been developed and presented in Scheme 1 . The tris part played as the carbon source and the glycine moiety was acted as surface functionality. Glycine was attached to the exterior surface of the nanoparticles through amide linkages imprinted in situ by thermal condensation among the ammonium carboxylate groups (viz. NH3 +−OOC-→−NHCO-+H2O) [52]. Zeta potential of the C-dots was found to be −20.8mV which confirmed the formation of highly stable anionic type C-dots. Using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 as a reference, the quantum yield value was calculated to be 42%.

The formation of C-dots having a major abundance of 3nm in average size was confirmed from HRTEM micrographs as shown in Fig. 1 (a-c). The HRTEM images clearly disclose that all C-dots are spherical in shape with a narrow size distribution in the domain of 1.5–5nm (Fig. 1a-inset). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) as shown in Fig. 1d, revealed the amorphous nature of the TG-CDs. The immobilized C-dots on wafer surface were observed by AFM. The 2D image is shown in Fig. 1e and the 3D image is shown in Fig. 1f. The average surface roughness (Rav) and root mean square surface roughness (Rrms) are detected to be 3.85nm and 4.08nm respectively. The depth profile plot in Fig. 1e (inset) confirms the size distribution of TG-CDs. The density of TG-CDs is approximately 1012/cm2. The elemental composition of the synthesized C-dots was determined from selected area EDX (Fig. 1 g), the mass and atomic ratios of carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) of the carbogenic dots were found to be 43.58:28.85:27.57 and 48.96:27.79:23.25 respectively.

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1 h) displays several common features for C-dots. Peaks at 3200–3550cm−1 and 1610cm−1 are present for OH stretching vibration and carbonyl (CO) characteristic stretching vibration respectively. Peak at 1455cm−1 was assigned as the CN stretching vibration of C-dots. The characteristic peak of NH was merged with the OH peak which showed broadening of the region [11,53]. The presence of polar groups in the C-dots suggests that the dots are very prone to water. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1i) of the TG-CDs appearances a broad peak at nearly 26° (002) which can propose the poor crystalline nature and graphene like structure of the TG-CDs [54,55].

The presence of C, N and O is supported from the XPS measurements of TG-CDs (Fig. 2 a). The high resolution spectra (Fig. 2b) reveal the C1s peaks at 284.4, 285.3, 287.2 and 288.6eV can be ascribed to the carbon in form of CC (sp3), CN (sp3), CO (sp2) and OCO (sp2) respectively [56,57]. The N1s peaks at 400.0eV and 401.6eV also elaborated in Fig. 2c which confirm the N is the state of CN (sp3) and NH (sp3) respectively [58]. These results can confirm the synthesis of N-doped CDs. The O1s peaks at 531.7 (CO) and 533.2eV (COH/CO-C) also documented in Fig. 2d [58]. High amount of polar functionalities in the carbogenic dots implies its high degree of water soluble nature in ambient temperature. The Raman scattering experiment of TG-CDs was also done but the high fluorescence feature of these CDs dominates over Raman signal. Thus no prominent feature were monitored for this TG-CDs[52,59]

The characteristic identity of the C-dots is their photoluminescent (PL) behaviour. The absorption peak at 232nm was assigned to the π-π* transitions of the CC bond (Fig. 3 a). In addition to the π-π* transitions at 232nm, the broad absorption peak at 350–375nm was probably associated with the generation of excited defect surface states produced by N heretoatoms, which could cause strong luminescence [60–62]. The synthesized C-dots in aqueous solution exhibited blue luminescence on exposure of UV light as shown in the inset of Fig. 3a. The PL spectra of C-dots with variation of pH were recorded (Fig. 3b). The C-dots exhibited strong PL peak in physiological pH range (4–8). This is due to the n-π* and π-π* electronic transitions which are aftereffects of refilling or depletion of their valance bands with alteration of pH. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, quantum yield was calculated after excitation of C-dots at 350nm.

Excitation dependent PL spectra (Fig. 3c) unveil the shift of PL peak from 504nm to 570nm after excitation of the TG-CDs in the range from 330 to 450nm. The PL behaviour was may be a cause of surface defects by polar functionalities and non-negligible impact of suspending bonds engrossed at the surface of C-dots. According to Xu et al. emissions of C-dots was primarily originated from continuous surface asperities and several faulty states which were responsible for emissions after excitations in different wave lengths [63]. The fluorescence decay profile of TG-CDs has been represented in Fig. 3d. The fluorescence lifetime of TG-CDs was calculated to be 10.1ns after fitting of the decay profile data.

The performed salt effect on the fluorescence activity of TG-CDs were performed in 0.6M KCl solution and depicted in Fig. 3e. It is shown from the figure that the PL intensity sharply increases with increasing KCl salt concentration from 0M to 0.6M. But when the KCl concentration increases from 0.6 to 2.0M the PL intensity remains almost invariable. The fluorescence activity in salt solution is assigned to the fact that the ionic strength of KCl solution can efficaciously inhibit the agglomerated lump formation of TG-CDs and distinctly disunite TG-CDs under salt interfaces. This study can support the stability and keeping strong fluorescence activity under rigorous salt concentration.

The fluorescence generating surface ligand i.e. glycine has significant effect on fluorescent intensity. Thus in searching for better fluorescence property, the amount of surface ligands was altered. The Fig. 3f showed that with increasing the glycine content the FL intensity increased. Thus tris: glycine=1:2.5 was fixed as the optimized ratio to get desirable fluorescence characters.

In terms of stability, Fig. 4 a depicts the compared fluorescence spectra of TG-CDs after 35days. As per Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensities of freshly prepared TG-CDs and TG-CDs stored for 35days were almost invariable which implies its fluorescent stability. In this context, the stability was also judged by another atmospherically abundant molecule, oxygen; because oxygen itself can acts as a molecular collisional quencher. In order to obtain reliable measurement results, the dissolved oxygen test in the TG-CDs solution have been performed. 3mL TG-CDs solution was purged by continuous flow of nitrogen for 20min. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of TG-CDs is almost unchanged before and after removing the dissolved oxygen by nitrogen purging. Hence, as an outcome from our dissolved oxygen experiment, it can be concluded that the effect of dissolved oxygen in the fluorescence activity can be neglected.

In Fig. 5 a plausible mechanistic pathway of TG-CDs formation have been schematically shown. As shown in the mechanism, it might be that tris molecules as a precursor of carbogenic core of TG-CDs, were primarily aromatized by intermolecular dehydration followed by “step growth type polymerisation” process. Later on, the tris molecules were tended to carbonize and generated carbon nuclei which were primarily an island of sp2 hybridised carbons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TG-CDs based chemosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the severe threat of copper ions on human health and environment, it is definitely important to establish a promising sensing method which can selectively and sensitively monitor Cu2+level in aqueous system. From the results of the fluorescence property mentioned above, the as-prepared TG-CDs meet all the requirements to be a fluorescent nano-probe. Hence, we have explored TG-CDs-based sensor for efficient detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity as a chemosensor for Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the sensitivity of the TG-CDs chemosensor, different concentrations of the copper ions ranging from 0 to 300μM were added to the aqueous dispersion of the C-dots. In a typical run, aqueous solution of the TG-CDs (200μL) was added into 10mL calculated amount of various concentrations of Cu2+solutions. Based on the luminescence properties of the C-dots, FL quenching behaviour as a function of analyte concentration was measured at λex =350nm at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of the TG-CDs was gradually decreased with increasing concentration of the copper ions at around 480nm. This reveals that luminescence property of the TG-CDs is sensitive to Cu2+ concentration. The time dependent FL spectra (Fig. 6b) show 10min was required to complete the reaction between TG-CDs and Cu2+. To get insight into the quenching phenomenon of the TG-CDs by Cu+2, FL quenching data were analyzed by Stern-Volmer equation. Generally quenching mechanism can be explained by both (a) dynamic quenching and (b) static quenching. Dynamic quenching originated from collisional interaction between the quencher and the fluorophore. For this type of quenching the Stern-Volmer equation can be described as follows [64].
(2)
F
0
F
=
τ
0
τ
= 1 +
k q
τ
0
[ Q ]
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. τ and τ 0 are the fluorescence lifetime in the presence and absence of the quencher respectively. k q is the rate constant of dynamic (collisional) quenching, [Q] is the quencher concentration. Another type of quenching i.e. static quenching occurs when a non-fluorescent complex formed between the fluorophore and the quencher. It can be described by the following Stern-Volmer equation
(3)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K SV
[ Q ]
where K SV is the Stern-Volmer constant. In static quenching the lifetime of the fluorophore is unperturbed and hence
τ 0
τ
= 1
[64]. The plot (Fig. 6c) shows the dependence of F/F0 on the concentration of the quencher i.e. Cu2+ ions over the range 0–300μM. In the present case, the plot shows a degree of non-linearity which indicates both dynamic and static quenching (turn-off) occurs in the mechanism of interaction. Inset of Fig. 6c corresponds to the relationship between log F 0/F and concentration of Cu2+ions (from 0 to 12μM). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.32μM using the three times standard deviation rule (LOD=3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and s is the slope of the calibration curve).

The pH of the aqueous dispersion of the TG-CDs plays important role in this sensing probe. In this present sensing system, FL intensity of the C-dots in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions for both the cases are pH dependent. In strong acidic medium (pH less than equal to 3), fluorescence intensity of the TG-CDs is comparatively low and thus incompatible for developing a stable sensing probe. Moreover, the FL intensity becomes feeble at the pH value greater than 9. In contrast, TG-CDs have strong FL intensity in the pH range 4–6 shown in Fig. 6d.Upon addition of Cu2+ ions fluorescence quenching (turn-off) can be observed in the pH range 2–12 (blue column in Fig. 6d). In strong acidic medium, the lone-pair present on the nitrogen functionalities at the surface of the TG-CDs become protonated and are unable to form cupric amine complex. In alkaline medium, partial hydrolysis of copper (Cu2+) ions hinder the complexation reaction between the nitrogen functionalities of the TG-CDs and copper ions. This indicates that weakly acidic condition might be stable and suitable for this sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the TG-CDs for Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of this sensing probe, 50μM of the various metal ions including Cd2+,Mn2+,Ba2+,Hg2+,Fe2+,Ag+,Ca2+,Li+,Na+,K+ Mg2+,Pb2+,Zn2+ and Cu2+ were added to the 200μL aqueous solution of the TG-CDs. A much lower FL signal (Fig. 6e) was observed for the TG-CDs upon addition of Cu2+ ions. Whereas, no noticeable decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed for the other metal ions. The elevated selectivity of these TG-CDs for Cu2+ ions is due to greater thermodynamic affinity and rapid chelation process with ‘N’ and ‘O’ of the TG-CDs compare to other metal ions [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of TG-CDs based Cu2+ sensor in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The high selectivity and excellent sensitivity of the TG-CDs for Cu2+ ions suggest that this chemosensor might be applicable for Cu2+ detection in real water samples. To evaluate this TG-CDs based Cu2+ sensor in an artificial system, the performance of this chemosensor probe for real water sample analysis was investigated by water samples obtained from Ganga River, West Bengal, India. We applied a standard addition method to detect the concentration of Cu2+ ions in river water [49]. Before the analysis, the river water samples were filtered through a 0.22μm membrane and centrifuged at 12000rpm for 20min. The water samples were then spiked with different concentrations level of Cu2+ ions and then examined using the proposed method as shown in Fig. 7 a. The FL signal of the TG-CDs gradually decreased with increasing concentration of the Cu2+ ions from 0 to 100μM when excited at 350nm. Fig. 7b shows the dependence of F/F 0 on the concentration of the Cu2+ions over the range 0–100μM. Inset (Fig. 7b) shows the relationship of log F 0/F and Cu2+concentrations from 0 to 15μM. It can be seen that a good linear correlation have been found over the above concentration range for river water samples. In spite of the possible hindrance of several organics and minerals in the river water, this sensing platform can still act efficiently for the sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ions. This result implies that TG-CDs based sensor can be a potential sensing system for the selective detection of Cu2+ions in practical applications.

Generally, quenching mechanisms include inner filter effects, electron transfer processes, ion binding interaction and non-radiative recombination pathways [65]. In this present case, the decrease of fluorescence intensity of the TG-CDs in the presence of copper ions may be due to the formation of cupric amine complex at the surface of the TG-CDs. Cu2+ being a paramagnetic ion have d9 system in its electronic configuration with one unfilled d shell and hence can quench the FL signal of the TG-CDs via electron or energy transfer [66]. It is believed that the chelation process of the copper ions with the oxygen and nitrogen at the surface of the carbogenic dots fetch them close in proximity leading to the considerable decrease in fluorescence intensity [67]. Fig. 7c shows the PL spectra of the aqueous dispersion of TG-CDs under different conditions (in presence and in the absence of 50μM copper ions). A strong FL response of the TG-CDs can be obtained at 480nm when excited at 350nm. Upon addition of 50μM Cu2+ions the FL intensity was tremendously decreased indicating that copper ions can effectively reduce the FL of the TG-CDs. In presence of same molar amount (50μM) of strong metal ion chelator such as Na2EDTA, the Cu2+quenched aqueous dispersion of the TG-CDs shown in Fig. 7c. From the spectra it can be seen that the FL intensity of the solution was almost completely restored compare to the TG-CDs solution. It implies that Na2EDTA (a strong hexadentate metal ion chelator) can prevail over the TG-CDs and undergo complexation reaction with copper ions [68].

The comparison between our work and by several researchers was tabulated in Table 1 . Table 1 showed that the detection limit of our prepared carbon dots as chemosensing application (for Cu2+ ions is aqueous medium) is better than some previous works. This means our fluorescent carbon dots can be applied as a superior chemosensor for Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic productions of TG-CDs assisted AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The TG-CDs were mixed and incubated with silver nitrate solution in alkaline environment for 5min. Gradual transformation of the colour from pale yellow to bright yellow indicated the formation of AgNPs. The UV-spectra of the AgNPs (Fig. 8 a) showed absorption peak at 410nm which confirm the generation of silver nanoparticles but in the control reaction (i.e. without TG-CDs) there was no visual change in colour as well as UV-absorption spectra. To avoid the chance of photoreduction of Ag+ to Ag, the reaction was carried out in dark without C-dots which results no colour change and also no absorption peaks in UV-spectra. However, in the presence of C-dots, gradual colour change was observed within 5min and recorded by UV–vis spectroscopy. Such observation can indicate that catalytic reduction of Ag+ to AgNPs was irrespective of photoirradiation. The prepared AgNPs was also stable for a long time (6 weeks without changing peak intensity). (Supporting Information S1)

HRTEM image in Fig. 8b confirms the generation of well-dispersed AgNPs. The HRTEM cannot recognize the distinct carbon dot particles because of their low contrast on the substrate used. Similar evidence was reported by Pd particle graphene quantum dots composites[80] and chitosan derived carbon dot stabilized AgNPs [81]. The average size of the AgNPs was in the region of 2–8nm (by statistical analysis using image J based on 200 nanoparticles in one frame) which are much smaller than previously reported results by vitamin C [82,83]. HRTEM also showed high crystallinity of the AgNPs with a distinct lattice of 0.242nm (inset of Fig. 8b) which attributed to Ag(111).

To recognize the preparation of the silver nanoparticles, UV–vis spectra are very much trustworthy in such context. Fig. 9 a showed UV–vis spectra of the AgNPs with different TG-CDs solution which resulted a bathochromic shift of the λmax with monotonous decrement in absorption intensity. The gradual shift of absorption maxima or λmax corresponds to the size increment of the AgNPs. The hydrodynamic diameter of the AgNPs was calculated from the dynamic light scattering (DLS), which showed a gradual enhancement in diameter of AgNPs from 32 to 122nm with increasing the TG-CDs concentration in reaction mixture from 45 to 106mgL−1. From the DLS data (Supporting Information S2) it can also be speculated that the synthesized AgNPs were adhered on the surface of the carbon dots surface instead of isolated and distinct from each other. Another experiment was also done with increasing the pH of the medium (Fig. 9b) which exhibited that mild alkalinity is favourable for the nucleation of AgNPs. Another factor which also has a significant impact on the growth of AgNPs, i.e. the surface charge of the TG-CDs. The zeta value showed the negative surface charged character of the TG-CDs. The negative charges of the TG-CDs have an affinity to attach with the Ag+ ions. Mild alkaline conditions sustain the environmental feasibility to coordinate the Ag+ ions and finally reduces to Ag(0) therein. Surface polar groups basically OH, −NH2, −COOH, phenolic groups are essential for surface anchoring and reduction of metal ions in nano-carbon support [84,85]. The surface charges have also a stabilizing impact on AgNPs formation. When Ag+ ions were adhered to the TG-CDs surface, the surrounding surface charges help to prevent aggregation of the AgNPs. The catalytic activity was also monitored with increasing temperature and incubation time. It was seen from the temperature and time dependency plot (as shown in Supporting Information S3), which showed that with increasing temperature and time the absorbance value increases upto a certain point, after that the absorbance value become fixed, i.e. the AgNPs population become almost constant.

The photoluminescence plot of the obtains TG-CDs reduced AgNPs (Supporting Information S4) showed a relatively lowering of photoluminescence intensity and a blue shift was observed of approximately 12nm for the excitation-emission maxima. As it is known that the photoluminescence is an outcome of radiative combination of hole and electron bounded to the peripheral region of TG-CDs, the photoluminescence behaviour can imply that the surface character of TG-CDs has been affected by the reduction and in situ growth of AgNPs on the TG-CDs surface.

Another evidence in favour of the AgNPs synthesis was also been justified by resonance light scattering (RLS) study at a fixed wave length of 360nm (Supporting Information S5). The RLS of the catalytically reduced AgNPs was noticed as of increasing AgNPs concentration. The linear relationship plot between ΔRLS and Ag+ ion concentration within a range between 0 and 8μM has shown in Fig. 10 . A linear trend-line fitting was done to get the linear regression equation of ΔRLS=193.534×[Ag+] − 50.175 with a goodness of fit value of 0.99836.

In summary, we developed a strongly blue luminescent nitrogen doped, carbon rich carbogenic dot using tris and glycine as precursor materials. The TG-CDs have immense water solubility, withstanding salt solution effect and pH-dependent property. The fast response in metal detection can make the C-dots a potential nanosensor probe. For the TG-CDs in this present work, the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be about 0.32μM which is much lower than the safe limit (20μM) set up by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) for Cu2+ ions in drinking water. This indicates that the TG-CDs based chemosensor can be a promising sensing platform for detection of Cu2+ ions in drinking water. Besides this chemosensor type activity these carbon dots also have a potential reducing power to synthesize silver nanoparticles in in-situ. The derived AgNPs were also stabilized by the surface charges of TG-CDs up to 6 weeks. Hence, our prepared fluorescent C-dots have dual activity of metal sensing as well as stabilized nanoparticle.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.06.068.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Bioimaging of hyaluronic acid derivatives using nanosized carbon dots

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sayan Ganguly was born in the India in 1988. He obtained his B.Sc degree in Chemistry (honours) in 2009 at Ramakrishna Mission Vidyamandira, Belur Math, University of Calcutta; and then his post-graduation B.Tech in 20012 (University of Calcutta). After that he obtained M.Tech in 2014 from University of Calcutta in polymer science and technology specialization. Then he joined to Prof. N C Das research group in Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, India. His primary research interests include superabsorbent hydrogels, fluorescence in hydrogel, nanomaterials in hydrogels, and hydrogels in biomedical fields.

Poushali Das was born in the India in 1991. She obtained her B.Sc degree in Chemistry (Honours) in 2013 from Serampore College, University of Calcutta. After that she obtained M.Sc in 2015 from Sree Chaitanya College affiliated to West Bengal State University in Chemistry specialization in Inorganic Chemistry. Then she joined to Prof. N C Das research group in Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, India. Her primary research interests include multifunctional luminescent carbon dots and its application in diverse fields, hybrid nano-composites based on carbon-dots.

Madhuparna Bose was born in India in 1988. She has done her Bachelor’s in Chemistry (Honours) from Presidency College, University of Calcutta in 2009 and Master’s in Biochemistry with specialization in Structural Biology from University of Calcutta in 2011. Currently she is pursuing her Ph.D in the Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur. Her research mainly focuses on the chromosome segregation proteins of Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

Subhadip Mondal was born in the India in 1987. He obtained his B.Sc degree in Chemistry (honours) in 2008 at Midnapore College, Vidyasagar University; and then received his post-graduation M.Sc degree in Chemistry 2010 (Vidyasagar University). After that he received M.Tech degree in 2013 from Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, India. Then, he joined to Prof. N. C. Das research group in Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, India. His current research interests include Conductive polymer nanocomposites and its application in electromagnetic interference shielding.

Amit Kumar Das was born in India in 1964. He has obtained his B.Sc in Chemistry (honours) in 1985 from Midnapore College, University of Calcutta and M.Sc in Pure Chemistry with specialization in Physical Chemistry in 1987. He has obtained his Ph.D in X-ray crystallography in Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics, University of Calcutta in 1995. His post-doctoral research topic included structural biology of protein phosphatases which was pursued in University of Oxford, UK. He has joined Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur in 1998 as an assistant professor. Currently he is a professor (HAG) in the Department of Biotechnology, IIT Kharagpur.

N. C. Das was born in India in 1970. He received his B. Sc. degree (1992) in Chemistry (honours) from Bagnan College, University of Calcutta and B.Tech. degree (1996) in Polymer Science and Technology from Calcutta University. Subsequently, he completed his M. Tech. degree (1998) in Rubber Technology and Ph.D (2002) in polymer field from Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, India. He carried out his post-doctoral research at Hiroshima University, Japan and Michigan Technological University, USA. He worked as a Research Associate, SUNY Binghamton, USA. He also worked as a Research Professor at Indiana University, Bloomington, USA. He joined Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur in 2015 as an Associate Professor. His research interests are in the field of nanomaterials, thermoplastics Elastomers, Polymer nanocomposites, rubber, food packaging, SAXS and SANS
